title: Effects of proton pump inhibitors on gastrointestinal bleeding and cardiovascular outcomes in myocardial infarction patients treated with DAPT.
gap_statement: A discrepancy exists between European and American guideline recommendations for routine use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) in patients on dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT), leaving clinicians without clear evidence on whether PPI co-prescription improves the bleeding-ischaemia trade-off after AMI in East Asian populations.
framing_pattern: International guideline inconsistency + missing real-world East Asian evidence; framed as a need to 'determine the association between the co-prescription of PPIs and DAPT and the occurrence of gastrointestinal bleeding and ischaemic events' in nationwide AMI/PCI patients.
hypothesis: Co-prescription of PPIs with DAPT after PCI for AMI is associated with reduced major gastrointestinal bleeding without increasing major adverse cardiac and cerebrovascular events (MACCE).

methodology_choice.data_source: Nationwide Korean claims database of AMI patients undergoing PCI with DAPT (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06241833)
methodology_choice.rationale: Large-scale propensity score matching on nationwide claims was used to emulate guideline-relevant comparisons in real-world East Asian patients where randomized evidence is lacking.

key_finding: After propensity-score matching of 35,560 pairs, PPI co-prescription was associated with significantly lower 1-year major gastrointestinal bleeding (0.7% vs 0.4%, HR 0.59, 95% CI 0.48-0.73) without a difference in MACCE (HR 0.98, 95% CI 0.94-1.02), consistent across P2Y inhibitor type, PPI type, and bleeding-risk strata.

title: Combustible cigarette smoking harms nearly every organ in the body.
gap_statement: Public and clinical discourse continues to underestimate the multi-organ harms of combustible cigarette smoking, motivating an editorial framing that smoking is not only a cardiovascular or pulmonary issue but a whole-body health threat.
framing_pattern: Editorial framing of smoking harm as systemic ('nearly every organ in the body'); positions combustible cigarettes as the reference baseline against which alternative nicotine products must be judged.

methodology_choice.data_source: European Heart Journal editorial commentary (no primary data)
methodology_choice.rationale: Editorial format used to contextualize accompanying empirical work on smoking and cardiovascular outcomes for the journal's readership.

key_finding: Combustible cigarette smoking is reaffirmed as harmful to nearly every organ system, and clinicians and policy makers should treat it as a systemic disease driver rather than an organ-specific risk factor.

title: Prognosis after switching to electronic cigarettes following percutaneous coronary intervention: a Korean nationwide study.
gap_statement: Despite the increasing popularity of electronic cigarettes, the prognostic impact of switching to e-cigarettes among smokers with coronary artery disease after percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) remains unclear, leaving clinicians without evidence to counsel post-PCI smokers about harm-reduction options.
framing_pattern: Knowledge gap on real-world prognosis of post-PCI behaviour change; framed as 'the prognostic impact of switching to E-cigarettes ... remains unclear' and resolved using a nationwide post-PCI smoking-trajectory cohort.
hypothesis: Among smokers who undergo PCI, switching to e-cigarettes (especially exclusive use) is associated with lower major adverse cardiac events (MACE) than continued combustible cigarette use, with risk closer to that of successful quitters.

methodology_choice.data_source: Korean National Health Insurance nationwide cohort of 17,973 post-PCI smokers with pre- and post-PCI health screening (ClinicalTrials.gov NCT06338761)
methodology_choice.rationale: Nationwide linked claims and health-screening data allowed classification of post-PCI smoking trajectories (continued, switched to e-cig, dual user, quitter) at population scale, which is infeasible in trials.

key_finding: Over a median 2.4-year follow-up, switchers to e-cigarettes (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.69-0.98) and successful quitters (HR 0.87, 95% CI 0.79-0.96) had lower MACE risk than continued combustible smokers, and exclusive e-cigarette use was associated with lower MACE than dual use (HR 0.71, 95% CI 0.51-0.99).

title: Measuring patient-reported distress from breast magnetic resonance imaging: Development and validation of the MRI-related distress scale (MRI-DS).
gap_statement: Although breast MRI is a valuable screening tool, MRI testing burden is associated with cancer worry and reduced quality of life, yet existing PROMs such as STAI, HADS, and the Testing Morbidity Index are designed for general anxiety or generic HRQoL and 'are inadequate for assessing the MRI-related anxiety,' leaving no instrument able to capture comprehensive, breast-MRI-specific distress.
framing_pattern: Absence of a fit-for-purpose PRO instrument; framed as 'the absence of a suitable PRO measure (PROM) for evaluating patient-centered issues related to MRI,' addressed by following the COSMIN checklist to develop and validate a breast-MRI-specific scale.
hypothesis: A breast-MRI-specific PROM (MRI-DS) can be developed and validated to capture physical, psychological, practical, and injection-related distress that is distinct from general anxiety or HRQoL.

methodology_choice.data_source: Two-phase study at a Korean tertiary university hospital: Phase I cognitive interviews (n=8) and Phase II psychometric validation (n=180) at Samsung Medical Center Breast Cancer Center outpatient clinic (Apr-Aug 2023)
methodology_choice.rationale: COSMIN-guided two-phase design (item development with qualitative interviews followed by quantitative EFA/CFA and known-group analysis) was chosen to ensure both content and construct validity for an MRI-specific PROM.

key_finding: The 18-item, four-factor MRI-DS (physical, psychological, practical, injection-related distress) showed good factor structure, internal consistency (Cronbach alpha 0.82-0.91, total 0.91), discriminant validity (weak correlation with STAI and WHOQOL-BREF, moderate with QOL-CS-K fear-of-future-testing items), and known-group validity (higher scores in patients who rated MRI as the most burdensome breast examination); 97.2% of patients reported at least one troublesome MRI symptom.
limitations: Single-hospital convenience sample with few patients followed without breast cancer; no test-retest analysis was performed; convergent validity of physical, practical, and injection-related domains was not tested against separate instruments to limit respondent burden; CFI value was modest (0.881) because items capturing unique unmet needs were retained even when weakly correlated.
future_work: A future validation study should assess the MRI-DS in non-breast-cancer populations requiring breast MRI, and the scale should be applied to compare patient-reported impact of abbreviated MRI (AB-MRI) versus full dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI for shared decision-making.

title: Scalp Cooling in Preventing Persistent Chemotherapy-Induced Alopecia: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
gap_statement: Existing studies of scalp cooling efficacy are limited by short-term duration and have not evaluated whether scalp cooling prevents persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia (PCIA) months after chemotherapy ends.
framing_pattern: Methodological limitation of prior evidence ('current studies of the efficacy of scalp cooling are limited by short-term duration'), addressed by extending follow-up to 6 months post-chemotherapy with objective hair-thickness/density measurement.
hypothesis: Scalp cooling during chemotherapy reduces the incidence and severity of persistent chemotherapy-induced alopecia at 6 months after chemotherapy completion compared with usual care.

methodology_choice.data_source: Open-label 2:1 randomized controlled trial in newly diagnosed stage I-III breast cancer patients receiving neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy (Dec 2020-Aug 2021), with hair thickness and density measured by Folliscope 5.0 and CIA-related distress measured with CADS
methodology_choice.rationale: An RCT with objective Folliscope-based hair thickness/density assessment and validated CADS distress measurement was chosen to overcome the short-term, subjective limitations of prior scalp-cooling studies.

key_finding: At 6 months post-chemotherapy, PCIA occurred in 13.5% (12/89) of the scalp-cooling group versus 52.0% (26/50) of controls; scalp cooling increased hair thickness (mean difference 9.0 microns) and reduced CIA-related distress (CADS difference -3.2), with a small density difference (-3.3 hairs/cm).
future_work: Further research is necessary to observe longer-term benefits of scalp cooling beyond 6 months.

title: Emulating trial to evaluate the effectiveness of routine supportive care on mortality among cancer patients experiencing distress at the time of diagnosis.
gap_statement: Few studies have evaluated the effectiveness of interventions for distress during cancer diagnosis on clinical outcomes such as mortality in a real-world setting, leaving the value of routine information and psychosocial support for distressed newly diagnosed patients unproven.
framing_pattern: Real-world evidence gap on whether psychosocial care affects hard outcomes; framed as the need to test whether 'routine information and psychosocial support to patients experiencing distress at the time of diagnosis could decrease the risk of mortality,' addressed by emulating a target trial in routinely collected screening data.
hypothesis: Among newly diagnosed cancer patients with distress scores >=4 at screening, receipt of routine information and psychosocial support reduces 1-year and 3-year all-cause mortality compared with no such support.

methodology_choice.data_source: Retrospective cohort of 4,880 newly diagnosed cancer patients screened with tablet/kiosk-based distress assessment at a university-affiliated cancer center in Seoul, South Korea (Jul 2014-Dec 2017)
methodology_choice.rationale: A target-trial emulation with two groups (received vs did not receive supportive care) and Cox models was used to estimate causal effects on mortality in a real-world clinical setting where randomized trials of routine distress care are difficult.

key_finding: Receipt of routine information and psychosocial support was associated with a 27% lower 1-year mortality (HR 0.73, 95% CI 0.54-0.99), with a stronger effect in patients aged <60 (p for interaction 0.03), while the 3-year mortality reduction was non-significant (HR 0.93, 95% CI 0.76-1.14).

title: Comprehensive Young Age Breast Cancer registry from clinical, genomics, and patient-reported outcomes measured with 15 years follow-up: the CHARM cohort profile.
gap_statement: Young breast cancer (YBC) patients face distinct clinical challenges and research gaps that are inadequately captured by existing breast cancer registries, which typically lack integrated clinical, multi-omics, fertility-related, and patient-reported outcome data with long-term follow-up.
framing_pattern: Population-specific data gap; framed as 'distinct clinical challenges and research gaps in young breast cancer (YBC) patients,' addressed by establishing a dedicated prospective registry that integrates clinical records, multi-omics tissue/blood biobanking, reproductive hormones, and mobile PROs.

methodology_choice.data_source: Comprehensive Young Age Breast Cancer (CHARM) registry at Samsung Medical Center (since April 2013); patients <=40 at diagnosis or pregnant/postpartum within 1 year, with electronic medical record data, Medidata Rave EDC, central pathology review, annual bone density, multi-omics tumor/blood samples, reproductive hormone panels (AMH, estradiol, FSH, LH), and mobile PRO questionnaires
methodology_choice.rationale: A prospective cohort with integrated clinical, multi-omics, fertility, and PRO data was designed to address the lack of population-specific evidence and to enable 15-year follow-up tailored to YBC research priorities.

key_finding: The SMC YBC cohort enrolled 1,868 participants (mean age 35.57 years, 99.8% premenopausal), of whom 1,062 completed PRO questionnaires, establishing a comprehensive YBC platform to optimize care and knowledge for this underserved population.

title: Impact of Social Support during Diagnosis and Treatment on Disease Progression in Young Patients with Breast Cancer: A Prospective Cohort Study.
gap_statement: Although social isolation has been linked to mortality and recurrence in mixed-age cancer cohorts, the impact of changes in social support after diagnosis on clinical outcomes among young breast cancer patients (<=40 years) is not well understood, even though family and work demands make social support especially critical at this life stage.
framing_pattern: Population- and exposure-specific evidence gap; framed as 'the impact of social support decline after treatment on clinical outcomes among young patients (<= 40 years old) with breast cancer is not well understood,' addressed by examining change (not just baseline level) of social support in a young breast cancer registry.
hypothesis: Among young breast cancer patients, a decrease in social/family well-being (SFWB) between diagnosis and active treatment is associated with worse recurrence-free survival (RFS) compared with patients whose SFWB increases.

methodology_choice.data_source: Samsung Medical Center Young Breast Cancer Registry (NCT03131089), 2013-2021; 610 patients <=40 years with FACT-G Social/Family Well-Being measured before and during treatment
methodology_choice.rationale: A prospective cohort with repeated FACT-G measurements allowed modelling of change in SFWB and use of Cox regression with inverse probability censoring weights to estimate the association with RFS while controlling for clinical and sociodemographic confounders.

key_finding: Between diagnosis and during treatment, 53.8% of young breast cancer patients had decreasing SFWB; the decreasing group had a 2.49-fold higher risk of recurrence or death versus the increasing group (adjusted HR 2.49, 95% CI 1.04-5.98), with decreasing emotional family support showing the strongest association (HR 4.03, 95% CI 1.16-13.96); having children at diagnosis was the main risk factor for decreasing SFWB.
limitations: Most data were self-reported, exact biological mechanisms linking decreased SFWB to poor RFS could not be established, residual confounding from the observational design remains, distressed patients may have refused participation (underestimating change), and the cohort was Asian and well educated, limiting generalizability.
future_work: Further biological studies are required to confirm mechanisms (e.g., inflammation, oxytocin pathways), and health professionals should develop family-based psychosocial interventions including children and fathers to support young breast cancer patients.

title: Pretreatment endocrine symptoms and recurrence-free survival among young premenopausal patients with breast cancer: a prospective cohort study.
gap_statement: Although pretreatment vasomotor symptoms have been linked to breast cancer mortality in postmenopausal women, only a few studies have evaluated the association between pretreatment endocrine symptoms and clinical outcomes in premenopausal patients with breast cancer, in part because prior population-based cohorts contained too few young premenopausal cases to assess this question.
framing_pattern: Age- and menopausal-status-specific evidence gap; framed as 'only a few studies have evaluated the association between pretreatment endocrine symptoms and clinical outcomes among premenopausal patients with breast cancer,' addressed by leveraging a large young breast cancer registry with systematic FACT-ES at diagnosis.
hypothesis: Higher pretreatment endocrine symptom burden (measured by FACT-ES) is associated with worse recurrence-free survival in premenopausal breast cancer patients aged <=40 years.

methodology_choice.data_source: Samsung Medical Center Young Breast Cancer Registry (NCT03131089), 2013-2021; 977 premenopausal patients <=40 years with FACT-ES at diagnosis
methodology_choice.rationale: A large prospective young breast cancer cohort with standardized FACT-ES at diagnosis was used to overcome the small premenopausal sample sizes of prior studies, and Cox/Fine-Gray models adjusted for clinical and quality-of-life confounders to test endocrine symptoms as a prognostic marker.

key_finding: 94.7% of young premenopausal breast cancer patients reported at least one pretreatment endocrine symptom and 17.2% had at least one severe symptom; the high-symptom group had a 2.05-fold higher risk of recurrence or death than the low-symptom group (adjusted HR 2.05, 95% CI 1.19-3.54), with severe hot flashes (HR 5.63) and breast sensitivity/tenderness (HR 1.82) being key drivers, and symptom score correlated with the systemic immune-inflammation index.
limitations: Underlying mechanisms could not be established because hormone measurements were not available at diagnosis, residual confounding from the observational design is possible, and reverse causation cannot be excluded (some symptoms might reflect undiagnosed cancer), although the majority of participants had stage I/II disease detected by screening.
future_work: A multidisciplinary approach and further biological studies are needed to identify mechanisms linking pretreatment endocrine symptoms with prognosis and to establish optimal management strategies for young breast cancer patients.

title: Prediction Model for Postoperative Quality of Life Among Breast Cancer Survivors Along the Survivorship Trajectory From Pretreatment to 5 Years: Machine Learning-Based Analysis.
gap_statement: Existing prediction models for quality of life (QoL) in breast cancer survivors are limited because they (1) include only a few dimensions and miss key variables such as spiritual well-being, (2) rarely cover long-term survivors, (3) ignore the time-varying nature of QoL-related factors, and (4) lack interpretability and external validation, leaving clinicians without tools to tailor survivorship care across the trajectory.
framing_pattern: Multi-pronged methodological gap explicitly enumerated as four 'First/Second/Third/Fourth' limitations of prior ML models; framed as 'traditional statistical models have limitations in identifying factors associated with QoL over time,' addressed with an interpretable ML pipeline (SHAP) trained per survival phase with external validation.
hypothesis: An interpretable machine-learning pipeline using SHAP-based feature selection can predict poor QoL across different survivorship phases (pretreatment to 5 years) and identify time-varying patient-centered predictors that generalize to an external cohort.

methodology_choice.data_source: Development set: Breast Cancer Information Grand Round for Survivorship (BIG-S) cross-sectional study at Samsung Medical Center (2018-2019, n=6,265); external validation set: Beauty Education for Distressed Breast Cancer (BEST) longitudinal cohort at two Seoul university-based hospitals (2011-2016, n=432)
methodology_choice.rationale: Six ML algorithms (DNN, GBM, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, random forest) with SHAP-based recursive feature elimination, 10-fold cross-validation, and external validation on an independent cohort were chosen to overcome interpretability and generalizability limitations of prior QoL prediction models.

key_finding: The ML models achieved good performance across survivorship phases (AUC 0.808-0.860 in training; 0.770-0.862 in external validation); emotional function was most important pretreatment, physical function within 1 year post-surgery, fatigue between 1-4 years, and hopefulness was the single most influential feature across all phases.
limitations: Cross-sectional design means causal direction between QoL and symptoms/function could be reversed; QoL was measured with a single EORTC-C30 item (though validated); results may not generalize to other cancer survivors or settings.
future_work: Further studies in other cancer survivor populations are needed to confirm generalizability, and future research should focus on developing and testing interventions for physical function, fatigue, and hope-promoting psychosocial care across the survivorship trajectory.

title: Surveillance of Symptom Burden Using the Patient-Reported Outcome Version of the Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events in Patients With Various Types of Cancers During Chemoradiation Therapy: Real-World Study.
gap_statement: Existing symptom-measure guidelines for cancer were designed for clinical trials and provide little guidance for real-world surveillance; prior PRO-CTCAE subsets have focused on common Western cancers (breast, lung, colorectal) and lack coverage of less common cancers such as lymphoma, stomach, and liver cancer, and differences in symptom profiles across cancer types have not been fully elucidated for real-world surveillance.
framing_pattern: Mismatch between trial-oriented PRO guidance and real-world clinical needs; framed as 'a lack of guidance for practitioners and for performing surveillance in the real-world clinical setting' and the unmet need to characterize cancer-specific symptom profiles using cancer-tailored PRO-CTCAE subsets.
hypothesis: Symptom frequency and severity measured with cancer-specific PRO-CTCAE subsets differ by cancer type during chemotherapy or radiation therapy, and higher symptom burden is associated with poorer health-related quality of life.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional tablet-based PRO survey of 1,352 outpatients receiving chemotherapy and/or radiation therapy at the National Cancer Center (Goyang) and Samsung Medical Center (Seoul) between December 2017 and January 2018, using 10 cancer-specific PRO-CTCAE-Korean subsets developed via Delphi
methodology_choice.rationale: A two-center cross-sectional design with Delphi-derived cancer-specific PRO-CTCAE subsets and EORTC QLQ-C30 was used to capture real-world symptom heterogeneity across diverse cancer types and link PRO symptoms to HRQoL via linear regression.

key_finding: Gastrointestinal symptoms dominated across all cancer types; fatigue (76.5%), decreased appetite (65.4%), and numbness/tingling (57.5%) were the most frequent, with substantial site-specific patterns (e.g., decreased libido in >50% of colorectal, gynecologic, breast, and lung cancers; pain during sexual intercourse in 59.8% of gynecologic/lymphoma patients); worsening PRO-CTCAE scores were associated with clinically meaningful HRQoL declines (e.g., fatigue coefficient -8.15).
limitations: PRO reporting was voluntary, introducing potential participation bias (lower reporting in males and older patients); symptoms were captured only at outpatient visits, missing hospitalized or hospice patients who may be most symptomatic; treatment-modality effects were not assessed because of cohort heterogeneity; symptoms of unique cancers within broader categories were not separately described.
future_work: Holistic, multi-symptom monitoring and management strategies based on comprehensive PROs are needed in routine care, and the approach of linking routinely collected PROs to large population-based health care databases should be expanded.

title: Divorce after breast cancer diagnosis and its impact on quality of life.
gap_statement: Factors associated with divorce following breast cancer diagnosis and the consequences of such divorce for survivors' quality of life remain inadequately characterized, particularly in Asian settings, leaving clinicians without clear targets for early psychosocial intervention.
framing_pattern: Under-researched psychosocial sequela; framed as a need to 'identify factors associated with divorce following breast cancer diagnosis and measure the impact of divorce on the quality of life (QoL) of patients,' addressed through a multi-site outpatient survey of Korean survivors.
hypothesis: Sociodemographic factors (younger age, lower education, employment at diagnosis) are associated with higher likelihood of divorce after breast cancer diagnosis, and divorced survivors experience worse QoL, social functioning, and body image than those who remain married.

methodology_choice.data_source: Cross-sectional survey of 4,366 adult breast cancer survivors who completed active treatment without recurrence, collected at breast cancer outpatient clinics in South Korea (November 2018 to April 2019)
methodology_choice.rationale: A multi-clinic cross-sectional survey with logistic and linear regression was used to identify risk factors for post-diagnosis divorce and to compare QoL between divorced and married survivors in a large Korean sample.

key_finding: Approximately 11.1 per 1,000 married breast cancer survivors experienced divorce after diagnosis; younger age, lower education, and being employed at diagnosis were risk factors; divorced survivors reported significantly lower overall QoL (coef -7.50), social functioning (coef -9.47), and body image, and more pain, insomnia, financial difficulties, and hair-loss-related distress.
future_work: Identifying risk factors of divorce will help ascertain the resources necessary for early psychosocial intervention in breast cancer survivors.
